content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


March 16, 2004


Tei Yukimoto, Deputy City Attorney
City of Fresno
2600 Fresno Street
Fresno, CA 93721-3602

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-04-031

Dear Ms. Yukimoto:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Council President Brad Castillo for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since you have not provided facts regarding the specific decision, we can only offer informal assistance.² This advice is applicable only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct and all of the material facts have been disclosed.³ The Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTIONS

1. Does Council President Brad Castillo have a conflict-of-interest disqualifying him from participating in a governmental decision including settlement negotiations between the City of Fresno and the owners of the Souza property?

2. If the legal analysis pertaining to Council President Castillo's possible conflict of interest depends on the value of the economic interests received, what is the valuation method for the worth of these economic interests?


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Informal assistance does not provide the official with the immunity conferred by formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).
³ In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77.

File No. 1-04-031
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

1. Council President Castillo has a conflict of interest if he has received gifts
totaling $340 or more from the Souzas in the preceding 12 months.

2. The valuation method for determining the worth of the economic interests at
issue is included in Step Three of the following analysis.

FACTS

Brad Castillo is a council member for the City of Fresno and in January of this
year, he was elected to serve as council president. For the past ten years or more, Council
President Castillo and his guests have been allowed to hunt doves annually, without
charge, for a three day period on the Souza property. The Souza property is real property
owned by Joe Souza Farms, a California limited partnership and Sanda Tavares, an
individual. The Souza property is not open to the public for hunting.

The Souza property is located next to a city landfill. The landfill is no longer in
use, and has been identified by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency ("EPA") as a
toxic waste site. The EPA has ordered the city to take remedial action to mitigate the
potential adverse effects on the environment. As part of its mitigation measures, the city
has installed groundwater monitoring wells on the Souza property, and will close three
agricultural wells on the Souza property. The city and the owners of the Souza property
are currently undergoing negotiations to remedy the groundwater contamination and to
compensate the owners of the Souza property for any damages caused by the
groundwater contamination.

Council President Castillo requests formal written advice to determine whether he
may participate in a governmental decision, including any settlement negotiations
between the city and the owners of the Souza property. Should the legal analysis depend
on the value of the benefits received, you would appreciate including in the advice letter,
the method for determining the worth of the value received.

Additional Facts

On the proceeding dates, you supplemented your written request for advice by
providing the Commission staff with the additional facts below.

February 24, 2004:

-       In regard to the governmental decision in question, because settlement
        negotiations are ongoing between the city and the owners of the Souza
        property, any information pertaining to these negotiations is confidential
        and cannot be disclosed.

File No. I-04-031
Page No. 3

-        It is your understanding that the Souza property is a limited partnership
and a family-owned business and not a Fortune 500 company.

February 25, 2004:

-        Council President Castillo last hunted on the Souza property on September 15,
2003.

-        Council President Castillo does not have any contractual agreement with the
owners of the Souza property for hunting purposes.

-        The owners of the Souza property are Joe Souza Farms, LP, a California
Limited Partnership, and Ludrie Souza as a limited partner.

-        The worth of the governmental decision is unknown at this time. Again, you
advised that any discussions pertaining to the settlement negotiations between
the city and the owners of the Souza property are confidential. Thus, details
of these negotiations were not disclosed to Commission staff.

March 8, 2004:

You provided Commission staff with background information relating to the
settlement negotiations between the city and the owners of the Souza property:

-        The EPA filed suit against the city in federal district court regarding the city
landfill. The suit resulted in a consent decree wherein the city was required to
take mitigation measures to control the contamination resulting from the
landfill. The city is undergoing settlement negotiations with the owners of the
Souza property to carry out the consent decree with the EPA. The settlement
negotiations may have been initiated to avoid litigation between the owners of
the Souza property and the city, but you are unaware of any impending
litigation between the parties at this time.

-        The city and the Souza property owners are dealing directly with one another
during these negotiations. It is likely that the negotiations will result in a
closed session in which the owners of the Souza property will be a named
party. The session may result in a settlement between the city and the owners
of the Souza property.

ANALYSIS

The primary purpose of the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions is to ensure that
"[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial

File No. I-04-031
Page No. 4

interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of
this goal, section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the public official has a financial interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step analytical framework for determining
whether the Act's conflict-of-interest restrictions apply to a given public official with
regard to a particular governmental decision (Regulation 18700(b).) We apply this
analytical process to your question.

Step One: Is Council President Castillo a "public official"?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; regulation 18701.) "Public official" is defined by the Act to
include "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency," with some exceptions that are not relevant here. (Section 82048.) "'Local
government agency' means a county, city or district of any kind including school district,
or any other local or regional political subdivision, or any department, division, bureau,
office, board, commission or other agency of the foregoing." (Section 82041.)

As a council member serving as council president for the city, Mr. Castillo is a
member and officer of a local government agency and is therefore a public official
subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.

Step Two: Will Council President Castillo be making, participating in making or
influencing a governmental decision?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply when a public official "make[s],
participate[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his [or her] official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he [or she] knows or has reason to know he
[or she] has a financial interest." (Section 87100; regulation 18700(b).) Commission
regulations describe in detail what constitutes making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision. (Regulations 18702.1-18702.3.)

Your inquiry presupposes that Council President Castillo will be at least
participating in any settlement agreement between the city and the owners of the Souza
property. Therefore, unless disqualified under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Act, we assume that Council President Castillo will be making, participating in making,
or influencing in a governmental decision.

Step Three: What are the possible sources of a financial conflict of interest? What
are the public official's economic interests?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the

File No. I-04-031
Page No. 5

official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests. Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5 list what constitutes an
economic interest under the Act.

The economic interest that pertains to your inquiry is described at section
87103(e) and defined under regulation 18703.4, which states, "[a] public official has an
economic interest in any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or
gifts aggregating three hundred forty dollars ($340) or more in value provided to,
received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made."

Under the Act, the term "gift" means, in pertinent part, "any payment that confers
a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater
value is not received and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value
unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the
public without regard to official status." (Section 82028(a).)

The Commission has generally recognized that acts of "neighborliness," although
conferring a theoretical economic benefit on an official, do not represent an economic
transaction that will bias the official in any real sense and thus, do not constitute
reportable gifts under the Act. (See e.g., In re Cory (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 153.) The
Commission has included the repairing of a fence by a neighbor, the offering of a ride,
and the fixing of a tire as examples of friendly acts of "neighborliness." (Cory, at p.
155.) For situations that lie in between acts of "neighborliness" and those wherein the
donor of the benefit is hoping for an economic return, the Commission has provided that
the following factors may be helpful in the determination of whether the benefit received
by the official is a gift rather than a non-reportable act of "neighborliness": 1) Whether
or not the benefit received is an expense that would normally qualify as a business
deduction on a tax return; 2) Whether or not the donor has, or in the foreseeable future
may have, business before the official who receives the service⁴; and 3) Whether or not
the service is normally the subject of an economic transaction². (See e.g. In re Stone
(1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 52.)

Under the facts you provide, we conclude that the benefit received by Council
President Castillo does not fall under the "acts of neighborliness" category. Even though
the Souza property is not open to the public for dove hunting, participation in this activity
is available elsewhere to the public at a cost and thus, the benefit conferred is the subject
of an economic transaction. Additionally, in light of the current negotiations between the

⁴ The absence of this factor does not mean that no gift has been provided. However, the presence of this
factor will in most cases provide strong, if not conclusive, evidence that a gift has been made. "It may be
that the donor has no intent of attempting to influence the official, but the need to avoid even the
appearance of possible impropriety is reason alone to require that a service provided to an official under
these circumstances be disclosed." (Stone, at p. 54.)
⁵ Giving a person occasional rides to work is not usually an economically based transaction whereas
participation in a car pool or the prolonged use of a car are generally economically based transactions.
Whether or not the donor and the official frequently reciprocate the service or the existence of emergency
circumstances are also relevant in applying this factor. (Stone, at p. 54, 55)

File No. I-04-031
Page No. 6

city and the owners of the Souza property, it is likely that the donor of the service, the
owners of the Souza property, will have business before the Council President Castillo in
the foreseeable future (if it is not precluded under the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Act). Thus, absent an exception, the hunting visits on the Souza property constitute
gifts under section 82028(a), because Council President Castillo was conferred a personal
benefit, in this case hunting doves annually on the Souza property, without paying
consideration of equal or greater value; in this case, free of charge.

Having determined that Council President Castillo's hunting trips constitute gifts
under the Act, we analyze whether this gift is an economic interest. As supplied in the
facts, Council President Castillo last hunted on the Souza property on September 15,
2003. Thus if Council President Castillo's makes, participates in making, or otherwise
influences a governmental decision regarding the Souza property within 12 months of
this date, the gift in question meets the time frame required by regulation 18703.4.

Pursuant to Regulation 18703.4, it is necessary to determine if the aggregate value
of the gift is $340 or more. The valuation of gifts is determined by regulation
18946(a), which provides that, except for certain exceptions that do not apply here, "gifts shall be
valued at fair market value as of the date of receipt or promise." In general, the fair
market value of an asset is the value that it would command in the open market. (See e.g.
In re Hopkins (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 107.) While the Act does not specifically set forth
how the fair market value of a gift is to be determined, one method would be to consider
the price of a comparable item to determine the value of the gift. (See generelly, In re
Hopkins, supra.) When the fair market value of the gift cannot be readily ascertained
because it is unique or unusual, the value shall be the cost to the donor, if known or
ascertainable. (Regulation 18946(b).) If the cost to the donor is unknown or not
ascertainable, the recipient shall make a reasonable approximation, taking into account
the price of similar items. If similar items are not available, a good faith estimate shall be
utilized. (Regulation 18946(b).)

Under the provided facts, it appears that the act of allowing Council President
Castillo to hunt doves on the Souza property for a three-day period could be valued by
considering the price of a comparable item. As previously discussed, although the Souza
property is not open to the public for dove hunting, participation in this activity is
available elsewhere. Thus, for valuation purposes, the hunting of doves on the Souza
property is not unique or unusual, since the value can be ascertained by comparing the
value of a similar activity. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it offers
advice of assistance. (Regulation 18329(b)(2)(b).) Therefore, if Council President
Castillo determines that his hunting visit of September 15, 2003, has an aggregate value
of $340 or more, it will constitute an economic interest under regulation 18703.4⁶.
Assuming Council President Castillo finds an economic interest, it is necessary to
continue the analysis of his potential conflict of interest under steps four through eight
below.

⁶ Please note that Council President Castillo may not receive more than $340 in gifts in a calendar year
from a single source. (Section 89503.)

File No. 1-04-031
Page No. 7

Step Four: Is the public official's economic interest directly or indirectly involved
in the governmental decision?

In order to determine whether a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest is material, it is necessary to determine if the
official's economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18704.)

Regulation 18704.1(a) provides:

"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision
before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by
an agent:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person."

In all other cases, business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts will be
considered indirectly affected by the decision for purposes of choosing the applicable
materiality standard under the Act. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)

As your facts provide, the City and the owners of the Souza Property are currently
directly dealing with each other to negotiate a settlement for damages to the Souza
Property. This negotiation, which is taking place to carry out the City's consent decree
with the EPA, is the proceeding in which the City and the owners of the Souza Property
are a party. Thus, the owners of the Souza Property are a named party in the proceeding
concerning the decision before the City and are therefore directly involved in the
governmental decision under regulation 18704.1(a).

Steps Five and Six: What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon the public
official's economic interests will meet this materiality standard?

Materiality Standard

As discussed above, a public official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision only if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on the official, a member of his or her immediate family or on any of the

File No. 1-04-031
Page No. 8

official's economic interests. (Section 87103.) A source of gifts to Council President
Castillo is deemed to be directly involved in the decisions you have described.
(Regulation 18704.1(a).) Further, regulation 18704.1(b) provides that if a source of a gift
is directly involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standard in regulation
18705.4(a) shall apply. Regulation 18705.4(a) provides that any reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a person who is a source of a gift to a public official, and which person
is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency is deemed material. This is
also known as the "one-penny" rule. (Fuller Advice Letter, No. 1-02-189.)

Foreseeability

Once a public official identifies the materiality standard that is appropriate to an
economic interest, he or she must then determine whether it is "reasonably foreseeable"
that the decision will result in a "material" financial effect on his or her economic
interests. A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if
it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met as a
result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be
certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Regulation 18706(b) provides a
non-exhaustive list of relevant factors that should be considered in determining the
foreseeability of a material financial effect on an economic interest.

Since the city and the owners of the Souza property are in settlement negotiations
for the mitigation of damages to the Souza property, it is reasonably foreseeable that the
settlement would have at least some financial effect on the property. This effect
would meet the strict standard of the so-called "one-penny" rule wherein any effect, even
as little as one penny, on the official's directly involved economic interest is deemed
reasonably foreseeable. (Fuller Advice Letter, supra.) Since it is likely that Council
President Castillo will find that material financial effects on his economic interests are
reasonably foreseeable as a result of the governmental decision in question, he is
disqualified from participating in the decision.

Steps Seven and Eight: "Public Generally" and "Legally Required Participation"
exceptions

An official who might otherwise have a conflict-of-interest in a particular
decision may participate in that decision if the circumstances are such that an exception
applies.

Step Seven includes the application of the "public generally" exception, which
applies where the reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect of a governmental
decision on the official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally. (Regulation 18707.) Step Eight includes the application of the "legally
required participation" exception that applies when the official is legally required to
participate in the decision pursuant to section 87101. (Regulation 18708(a).) Your facts
do not suggest that either of these exceptions is at issue.

File No. I-04-031
Page No. 9

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

[signature]
By: Marion Cruz
Intern, Legal Division

the Act). Thus, absent an exception, the hunting visits on the Souza property constitute
gifts under section 82028(a), because Council President Castillo was conferred apersonal
benefit, in this case hunting doves annually on the Souza property, without paylng
consideration of equal or greater value; in this case, free of charge.
Having determined that Council President Castillo's hunting trips constitute gifts
under the Act, we analYze whether this gift is an economic interest. As supplied in the
facts, Council President Castillo last hunted on the Souza property on September 15,
2003. Thus if Council President Castillo's makes, participates in making, or othernrise
influences a govemmental decision regarding the Souza property within 12 months of
this date, the gift in question meets the time frame required by regulation 18703.4.
Pursuant to Regulation 18703.4, it is necessary to determine if the aggregate value
of the gift is $340 or more. The valuation of gifts is determined by regulation 18946(a),
which provides that, excqrt for certain exceptions that do not apply here, "gifts shall be
valued atfanr market value as of the date ofreceipt orpromise." In general, the fair
market value of an asset is the value that it would command in the open market. (See e.g.
In re Hopkins (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 107.) While the Act does not specifically set forth
how the fair market value of a gift is to be determined, one method would be to consider
the price of a comparable item to determine the value of the gift. (See gorrerally,In re
Hopkins, supra.) When the fair market value of the gift cannot be readily ascertained
because it is unique or unusual, the value shall be the cost to the donor, if known or
ascertainable. (Regulation 18946(b).) If the cost to the donor is unknown or not
ascertainable, the recipient shall make a reasonable approximation, taking into account
the price of similar items. If similar items are not availablg a good faith estimate shall be
utilized. (Regulation I 89460).)
Under the provided facts, it appears that the act of allowing Council President
Castillo to hunt doves on the Souzaproperty for a three-daypenod could be valued by
considering the price of a comparable item. As previously discussed, although the Souza
property is not open to the public for dove hunting, participation in this activity is
available elsewhere. Thus, for valuation purposes, the hunting of doves on the Souza
property is not unique or unusual, since the value can be ascertained by comparing the
value of a similar activity. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it offers
advice or assistance. @egulation 18329OX2Xb).) Therefore, if Council President
Castillo determines that his hunting visit of September 15,2003,has an agregatevalue
of $340 or more, it will constitute an economic interest under regulation 18703.46.
Assuming Council President Castillo finds an economic interest, it is necessary to
continue the analysis of his potential conflict of interest under steps four through eight
below.
6 Please note that Council President Castillo may not receive more than $340 in gifts in a calendar year
from a single source. (Section 89503.)
Step Four: Is the public official's economic interest directly or indirectly involved
in the governmental decision?
In order to determine whether a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest is material, it is necessary to determine if the
official's economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision. (Regulation 187 04.)
Regulation 1 87 04. I (a) provides :
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision
before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by
an agent:
(1) Initiates theproceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject o{, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agerrcy.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a desision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other elrtitlement to, or contract with, the subject
petrson."
In all other cases, business entities, sources of incomg and sources of gifts will be
considered indirectly affected by the decision forpurposes of choosing the applicable
materiality standard under the Act. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)
As your facts provide, the City and the owners of the Souza Property are currently
directly dealing with each other to negotiate a settlement for damages to the Souza
Property. This negotiation, which is taking place to carry out ttre City's consent decree
with the EPA, is the proceeding in which the City and the owners of the Souza Property
xe apafiy. Thus, the owners of the Souza Property are a named party in the proceeding
concerning the decision before the City and are therefore directly involved in the
governmental decision under regulation 187 UaJ@).
Steps Five and Six: What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the linancial effect of the govemmental decision upon the public
official's economic interests will meet this materiality standard?
Materiality Standard
As discussed above, a public official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision only if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on the official, a member of his or her immediate family or on any of the
official's economic interests. (Section 37103.) A source of gifts to Council President
Castillo is deemed to be directlyinvolved in the decisions you have described.
(RegulationlST}aJ@).) Further,regulationl8704.l(b)providesthatifasourceofagift
is directlyinvolved in a governme,ntal decision, the materiality standard in regulation
18705.a@) shall apply. Regulation 18705.4(a) provides that any reasonably foreseeable
financial effect oo a person who is a source of a gift to apublic official, and which person
is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency is deemed material. This is
also known as the "one-penny''rule. (FullerAdvice Letter, No. I-02-189.)
Foreseeability
Once a public official identifies the materiality standard that is appropriate to an
economic interest, he or she must then determine whether it is 'teasonably foreseeable"
that the decision will result in a '?naterial" financial effect on his or her economic
interests. A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if
it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met as a
result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effectneed not be
certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere
possibility. Qn re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Regulation 187060) provides a
non-exhaustive list ofrelevant factors that should be considered in determining the
foreseeability of a material financial effect on an economic interest.
Since the city and the ownefti of the Souza property are in settlement negotiations
for the mitigation of damages to the Souza property, it is reasonably foreseeable that the
resulting settlement would have at least some financial effect on the property. This effect
would meet the strict standard of the so-called 'bne-penny''rule wherein any effect, even
as little as one penny, on the official's directly involved economic interest is deemed
reasonably foreseeable. (Fuller Advice Letter, supra.) Since it is likely that Council
President Castillo will find that material finansial effects on his economic interests are
reasonably foreseeable as a result of the governmental decision in question, he is
disqualified from participating in the decision.
Steps Seven and Eight: *Public Generally'and *Legally Required Participation,
exceptions
An official who might othemrise have a conflict-of-interest in aparticular
decision mayparticipate in that decision ifthe circumstances are such that an exception
applies.
Step Seven includes the application of the'lublic generally''exception, which
applies where the reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect of a governmental
decision on the official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally. (Regulation 18707.) Step Eight includes the application of the 'legally
required participation" exception that applies when the official is legally required to
participate in the decision pursuant to section 87101. (Regulation 1S70S(a).) Your facts
do not suggest that either of these exceptions is at issue.

QUESTION: 1. Does Council President Brad Castillo have a conflict-of-interest disqualifuing
him from participating in a governmental decision including settlement negotiations
between the City of Fresno and the owners of the Souza propert5f
2. If the legal analysis pertaining to Council President Castillo's possible conflict
of interest depends on the value of the economic interests received, what is the valuation
method for the worth of these economic interests?
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}1,4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections l8 109-
18997, ofthe California Code ofRegulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the official with the immunity conferred by fonnal written advice.
(Regulation 1 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed).
3 In re Oglesby (197 5) 1 FPPC Ops. 7l , 77 .


File No. I-O4-031

CONCLUSION: 1. Council President Castillo has a conflict of interest if he has received gifts
totaling $340 or more from the Souzas in the preceding 12 months.
2. T\e valuation method for determining the worth of the economic interests at
issue is included in Step Three of the following analysis.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 March 16, 2004 Tei Yukimoto, Deputy City Attorney City of Fresno 2600 Fresno Street Fresno, CA 93721-3602 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-04-031 Dear Ms. Yukimoto: This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Council President Brad Castillo for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since you have not provided facts regarding the specific decision, we can only offer informal assistance.² This advice is applicable only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct and all of the material facts have been disclosed.³ The Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions. QUESTIONS 1. Does Council President Brad Castillo have a conflict-of-interest disqualifying him from participating in a governmental decision including settlement negotiations between the City of Fresno and the owners of the Souza property? 2. If the legal analysis pertaining to Council President Castillo's possible conflict of interest depends on the value of the economic interests received, what is the valuation method for the worth of these economic interests? ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the official with the immunity conferred by formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed). ³ In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77. File No. 1-04-031 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION 1. Council President Castillo has a conflict of interest if he has received gifts totaling $340 or more from the Souzas in the preceding 12 months. 2. The valuation method for determining the worth of the economic interests at issue is included in Step Three of the following analysis. FACTS Brad Castillo is a council member for the City of Fresno and in January of this year, he was elected to serve as council president. For the past ten years or more, Council President Castillo and his guests have been allowed to hunt doves annually, without charge, for a three day period on the Souza property. The Souza property is real property owned by Joe Souza Farms, a California limited partnership and Sanda Tavares, an individual. The Souza property is not open to the public for hunting. The Souza property is located next to a city landfill. The landfill is no longer in use, and has been identified by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency ("EPA") as a toxic waste site. The EPA has ordered the city to take remedial action to mitigate the potential adverse effects on the environment. As part of its mitigation measures, the city has installed groundwater monitoring wells on the Souza property, and will close three agricultural wells on the Souza
